skill.name: deep-research
skill.description: Run Dify-powered deep research for coding and technical topics, returning task analysis and implementation guidance from an external workflow.
skill.summary: Use a Dify workflow to research technical tasks and produce structured implementation guidance.
skill.author: 21pounder

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but most shell execution and weak cryptography hits are false positives from template literals, Markdown fences, and ordinary text. The confirmed risks are legitimate but material: the skill runs a Bash-launched TypeScript client, loads .env files from parent directories, reads Dify credentials, and sends research prompts to a configurable external API.

medium_findings.title: External Research API Receives User Task Data and API Credentials
medium_findings.description: The client builds a configurable Dify API URL, reads DIFY_API_KEY, sends it in an Authorization header, and posts the research task and tech stack. This is intended behavior, but it exposes user prompts and credentials to an external service or any configured base URL.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call and bearer token header are directly visible, and the request body includes user-provided research inputs. The behavior is legitimate for a Dify client, so the risk is exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Parent Directory Environment File Loading
medium_findings.description: The script searches multiple parent and working-directory .env paths and loads the first matching file. This can unintentionally read project secrets outside the skill directory and bind the skill to credentials the user did not explicitly select.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dotenv path list and load loop are explicit. The paths are fixed rather than user-controlled, which lowers exploitability but does not remove the secret exposure concern.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Launched TypeScript Client Requires Command Execution Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and instructs the agent to run the TypeScript client with npx tsx. This is required for the skill, but users must trust the local script, installed package resolution, and command-line argument handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission and npx command are documented in the skill, and the script has a CLI entry point. The command target is specific, so this is an operational execution risk rather than evidence of arbitrary command injection.

medium_findings.title: Remote Workflow Output Can Influence Local Implementation Steps
medium_findings.description: The skill tells users to follow the returned guide for dependency installation, file creation, environment configuration, and verification commands. Because the guide comes from an external Dify workflow, users should review generated commands and code before applying them locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow handoff is clear in the documentation, and the listed guide sections can affect local files and commands. I did not find evidence that the remote workflow is malicious, so this remains a review-required supply-chain risk.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick hits correspond to TypeScript template literals, Markdown code fences, and sample commands. I did not find direct shell evaluation through backticks in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is visible and is not shell backtick execution. The actual command execution mechanism is Bash plus npx, which is captured separately.

low_findings.title: Fixed Parent Path Resolution Was Overclassified as Traversal
low_findings.description: The script resolves parent .env paths using constant relative paths. This is not user-controlled path traversal, but it is still relevant because it can load secrets from parent directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are hardcoded constants, so there is no attacker-controlled traversal input. The remaining concern is covered by the environment-file loading finding.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reviewed lines contain skill metadata and parameter descriptions, not cryptographic code. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain natural-language descriptions and JSON schema text. No cryptographic API or algorithm selection is present there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Combined Script Execution, Network Access, and Credential Use
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines Bash execution, external network calls, .env loading, and bearer-token authentication. This combination is expected for the Dify workflow, but it requires user trust and clear disclosure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Each component of the combination is directly visible in the files. I did not find prompt injection text or confirmed malicious exfiltration beyond the documented Dify API call.

content.value_statement: Complex technical work often needs current documentation, examples, and implementation planning. This skill sends a research request to a Dify workflow and returns structured analysis plus guidance.
actual_capabilities: Runs a Dify research workflow through a local TypeScript client.
Accepts a coding task, optional tech stack, and research depth.
Returns task analysis, knowledge gaps, and search-oriented research output.
Streams workflow progress and reports completion metadata.
Parses task analysis when the response contains JSON-formatted analysis.
Supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code skill environments.
limitations: Requires DIFY_API_KEY and access to the configured Dify API endpoint.
Sends research prompts and tech stack details to an external service.
Depends on npx, tsx, Node.js, and network availability.
Generated implementation guidance still requires human review before use.

use_cases.title: Plan an unfamiliar integration
use_cases.description: Research API requirements, common examples, and implementation steps before adding a new integration.

use_cases.title: Compare technical approaches
use_cases.description: Gather structured evidence about frameworks, libraries, or architecture options for a project decision.

use_cases.title: Prepare a literature-style review
use_cases.description: Collect sources and organize findings for a technical topic that needs deeper background analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Research a coding question
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with one clear topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research how to implement OAuth2 login in a Next.js application. Use React and TypeScript as the tech stack. Use depth 3.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate an integration plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Research how to add real-time notifications to a React and Node.js product. Compare WebSocket and Socket.io options. Use depth 4.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate architecture choices
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that needs comparison and tradeoffs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research database query optimization patterns for PostgreSQL and Prisma. Include indexing, query planning, and migration risks. Use depth 5.

prompt_templates.title: Create an implementation brief
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for actionable delivery guidance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research a secure file upload workflow for a SaaS app using Node.js, S3, and virus scanning. Produce risks, dependencies, and verification steps. Use depth 5.

output_examples.input: Research OAuth2 implementation for Next.js with TypeScript.
output_examples.output: A structured research report with task summary, required libraries, authentication flow decisions, implementation steps, verification guidance, and source references.

output_examples.input: Compare WebSocket and Socket.io for real-time notifications.
output_examples.output: A comparison of implementation complexity, scalability, client support, operational tradeoffs, and recommended next steps for the selected stack.

output_examples.input: Research Prisma query optimization for PostgreSQL.
output_examples.output: A technical guide covering likely bottlenecks, indexing options, query inspection methods, migration considerations, and validation steps.

best_practices: Review the task prompt for sensitive information before sending it to Dify.
Set DIFY_BASE_URL explicitly when using a private or enterprise Dify endpoint.
Treat generated commands and code as draft guidance until reviewed locally.
anti_patterns: Do not send secrets, customer data, or proprietary code unless the Dify endpoint is approved.
Do not run generated install or verification commands without checking their source and purpose.
Do not use maximum depth for simple questions when a quick research pass is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill work without a Dify API key?
faq.answer: No. The client requires DIFY_API_KEY before it can run the research workflow.

faq.question: Where does the research request go?
faq.answer: The client sends it to DIFY_BASE_URL, or to the default Dify API URL when no base URL is set.

faq.question: Can I use a private Dify deployment?
faq.answer: Yes. Set DIFY_BASE_URL to the approved private endpoint before running the skill.

faq.question: Does the skill modify project files by itself?
faq.answer: The client prints research output. The documentation asks the user or agent to review and apply the guide separately.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk marked medium?
faq.answer: It uses Bash, reads environment files, accesses API credentials, and sends prompts to an external service.

faq.question: What should I check before using the output?
faq.answer: Confirm sources, inspect generated commands, and verify that suggested dependencies match your security requirements.
